title: Mince pies
subtitle: Butter shortcrust cases filled with mincemeat, dusted with icing sugar
excerpt: Mince pies made with a rich butter shortcrust: cold butter rubbed into flour with a pinch of icing sugar and bound with egg yolk. The pastry bakes to a short, crumbly texture that breaks at the bite. Filled with mincemeat and dusted with icing sugar while warm.
sourceNotes: Mince pies are one of the oldest British baked goods, documented from the medieval period when they contained actual minced meat alongside dried fruit, spices, and suet. By the nineteenth century the meat had largely disappeared in domestic recipes, leaving the dried fruit and spice filling now standard. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a detailed recipe for mince pies using a minced-fruit mixture and shortcrust pastry. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records regional mince pie variations. The modern meat-free version with butter shortcrust pastry is fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked in the tin, then bag. Bake from frozen at 190°C fan for 25 to 28 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The pastry and filling scale well. Makes 12 per batch; double to fill two 12-hole tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake up to 3 days ahead. Warm at 160°C fan for 5 minutes and dust with fresh icing sugar.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, upper-middle shelf, 18 to 20 minutes until pale gold

glossaryTerms.definition: The three standard mince pie lid styles are: a flat pastry disc cut to the same size as the base (sealed with egg wash, pressed firmly); a star or tree cut-out pressed onto the top; and a criss-cross lattice of narrow pastry strips. The flat disc is the most forgiving for beginners and gives the most pastry per pie. Star and tree shapes are festive and expose the filling through the cutout. Whatever the lid style, chill the assembled pies for 15 to 20 minutes before baking to set the pastry and reduce shrinkage.

content.text: Good mince pie pastry is short and crumbly, not tough. The keys are cold butter, minimal handling, and a rest in the fridge before rolling. The mincemeat does the flavour work; the pastry's job is texture.

Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{icing-sugar}}. Beat the {{egg-yolks}} with 1 tablespoon of cold water and add to the flour. Bring together into a smooth dough without overworking it. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Combine the {{dried-currants}}, {{raisins}}, {{mixed-peel}}, grated {{apple-eating}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{brandy}}, and the zest and juice of the {{lemon}} in a bowl. Stir well. The filling can be used immediately or left overnight to allow the fruit to absorb the brandy.
Line the tin, fill, add lids
Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Grease a 12-hole bun tin. Roll the pastry to 3 mm and cut circles to line the holes. Fill each case with a heaped teaspoon of filling — just below the rim. See the
: cut lids or stars from the remaining pastry, press on firmly, and chill the assembled pies for 15 minutes before baking.
Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until the pastry is pale gold. Leave to cool in the tin for 5 minutes before lifting out. Dust generously with icing sugar while still warm.
